subtopics.subtopic_description: Educational background or training
notes: Holds a Master’s Degree, likely in Finance or Accounting
Completed undergraduate studies in Business Administration
Participated in continuing education courses in financial compliance and management
Completed in-house training for compliance and leadership at current employer

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific job title and role description
notes: Currently serves as Finance Manager at an Ivy League university in Massachusetts
Oversees financial operations for multiple academic programs
Manages a team of five finance coordinators
Acts as the main point of contact for cross-departmental financial matters

subtopics.subtopic_description: Current industry or sector (e.g., tech, finance, manufacturing)
notes: Works in higher education sector
Specializes in financial management within academia
Supports both internal university operations and external academic partnerships

subtopics.subtopic_description: Company size and environment
notes: Employed at a large Ivy League university with thousands of staff and students
Works in a collaborative, cross-functional environment
Finance department interacts regularly with administrative, academic, and external partners

subtopics.subtopic_description: Type of business or market segment
notes: Operates in nonprofit, higher education segment
Focus on program-based financial management and compliance
Supports both degree-granting and non-degree executive education programs

subtopics.subtopic_description: Duration/years of experience in current role
notes: Has more than 10 years of professional experience in financial management
Has held current finance manager role for several years
Prior roles included progressively increasing responsibility in finance

subtopics.subtopic_description: Professional seniority or career level
notes: Holds a senior management position within the finance department
Regularly oversees and mentors a team of professionals
Involved in strategic planning and process improvement for finance operations

subtopics.subtopic_description: Primary job responsibilities and regular daily tasks
notes: Manages budgeting, expense submission, approval, and financial reporting for multiple programs
Oversees invoicing, payment monitoring, and revenue booking
Prepares and reviews journal entries and financial reconciliations
Leads a finance team, providing task oversight and professional development
Supports hiring processes, manages compensation records, and ensures compliance

subtopics.subtopic_description: Approximate proportion of time spent on core activities
notes: Spends about 30% of time on financial processes and reporting
Allocates 25% of time to cross-team collaboration
Dedicates 50% of time to team management and oversight
Spends 10% on hiring, compensation, and HR support tasks
Devotes 5% to process improvement and strategy

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of autonomy and scope of decision-making in the role
notes: Operates with high autonomy in day-to-day financial management
Authorized to approve expenses and oversee financial processes
Makes independent decisions on team management, task prioritization, and process enhancements
Collaborates with leadership for strategic decisions and compliance policy changes

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that feel easiest or most natural to perform
notes: Financial reporting and budget reconciliation are routine and comfortable
Coaching team members and conducting regular check-ins come naturally
Processing invoices and tracking payments is handled efficiently

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks perceived as most challenging or complex
notes: Resolving discrepancies between financial systems and records
Managing cross-functional projects with tight deadlines
Ensuring compliance with evolving policies and regulations
Implementing new financial software or workflow tools

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are repetitive, data-heavy, or suitable for automation
notes: Invoice processing and payment tracking involve repetitive data entry
Reconciling large data sets for financial reporting is data-heavy
Routine email communication and status updates could be automated
Documenting standard operating procedures involves repetitive formatting

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are most enjoyable or engaging versus those that feel boring or tedious
notes: Enjoys problem-solving and optimizing financial workflows
Finds team coaching and development highly engaging
Considers repetitive data entry and manual reconciliation tedious
Routine compliance documentation is less stimulating

subtopics.subtopic_description: Common pain points or inefficiencies in completing tasks
notes: Manual reconciliation across platforms is time-consuming
Difficulty integrating data between Salesforce, Excel, and other tools
Delays from waiting on approvals or information from other departments
Duplicate data entry due to lack of system integration

subtopics.subtopic_description: How enjoyment, skill level, and productivity relate to one another
notes: Higher skill level in financial analysis leads to greater productivity and enjoyment
Repetitive or manual tasks reduce engagement and can impact efficiency
Enjoyment is highest when work involves collaboration and problem-solving
Productivity peaks when using well-integrated tools and clear processes

subtopics.subtopic_description: Attitude towards learning new technologies and tools
notes: Open and proactive about adopting new technologies
Views tech learning as essential for staying current in the field
Regularly seeks out new tools to improve workflow efficiency

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived adaptability to new software/methods
notes: Adapts quickly to new platforms such as LLMs and workflow tools
Has integrated multiple new systems (e.g., SharePoint, Workday) into daily work
Sees personal adaptability as a key professional asset

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness to invest time in tech training
notes: Willing to dedicate time to training if tool is relevant to job
Regularly participates in webinars and online tutorials for new software
Encourages team members to attend training sessions and share learnings

subtopics.subtopic_description: Motivations or barriers to learning new tech (e.g., workload, relevance)
notes: Motivated by potential time savings and process improvements
Barriers include high workload and competing priorities
Adoption is more likely if tech directly addresses pain points
Support from peers and leadership increases motivation to learn

subtopics.subtopic_description: Influence of peers or management on willingness to adopt new tools
notes: Peers often share success stories that encourage adoption
Management support and clear communication facilitate tool uptake
Collaborative culture promotes experimentation with new technologies

topic: Primary Tools and Technologies Used in Work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific software, platforms, or systems used daily
notes: Uses Salesforce CRM daily to track participant records and manage invoices
Relies on Excel and Google Sheets for budgeting, reconciliation, and journals
Communicates with team via Outlook and Slack
Uses SharePoint for document management and workflow tracking
Accesses Workday for HR tasks and payroll information

subtopics.subtopic_description: Essential non-AI tools for workflow
notes: Excel and Google Sheets are core for financial calculations and tracking
SharePoint is essential for document storage and version control
Outlook and Slack are primary channels for team and cross-department communication

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with industry-standard technologies
notes: Well-versed in Salesforce, Workday, Excel, and SharePoint
Experienced in using ePay, Buy2Pay, and TouchMe for financial transactions
Regularly updates skills to remain current with technology upgrades

subtopics.subtopic_description: Interoperability or integration issues between tools
notes: Manual data transfer is sometimes required between Salesforce and Excel
Lack of native integration between payment platforms and core finance systems
Version control challenges when multiple users update shared documents

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with fundamental AI/ML concepts and terminology
notes: Comfortable with basic AI/ML concepts such as large language models, automation, and data analysis
Regularly uses AI-powered tools for drafting communication and analyzing data
Understands the role of AI in automating repetitive financial tasks

subtopics.subtopic_description: Names of specific AI software/platforms currently used in work
notes: Uses LLM-based tools (such as ChatGPT or similar) for summarizing reports and drafting SOPs
Employs AI features in Salesforce and Outlook (e.g., predictive text, workflow automation)
Utilizes AI-driven data reconciliation tools where available

subtopics.subtopic_description: Frequency and purpose of AI tool application (specific use cases)
notes: Applies AI tools daily for data analysis, report generation, and communication
Uses AI to flag inconsistencies and automate reconciliation checks in financial data
Employs AI to draft routine emails and process documentation

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific examples of AI success and failure experiences (lessons learned)
notes: Successful automation of invoice tracking reduced manual entry errors
AI-generated emails save significant time but sometimes require manual review for tone
Experienced occasional mismatches in data categorization by AI, requiring oversight
Learned that clear input prompts are critical for reliable AI output

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of organizational training or peer resources for AI use
notes: Company provides occasional AI tool training sessions and webinars
Peers share tips and best practices for AI tool usage
Access to internal knowledge base for troubleshooting AI-related issues

topic: AI Interaction Style and Workflow Change

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preferred mode of interaction: independent versus step-by-step collaboration
notes: Prefers step-by-step collaboration with AI for complex or high-stakes tasks
Uses AI independently for routine or repetitive activities
Adjusts interaction style based on task urgency and complexity

subtopics.subtopic_description: Style of human-AI teaming (e.g., advisor, assistant, co-worker)
notes: Views AI primarily as an assistant for automating routine work
Relies on AI as an advisor for data analysis and report generation
Sees AI as a co-worker that supports, but does not replace, human oversight

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness and openness to adopting new AI-driven workflows
notes: Highly open to piloting AI-driven process improvements
Encourages team adoption of AI tools where they add value
Actively seeks new AI features relevant to financial operations

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preference for conversational vs. command-based interfaces (communication dynamics)
notes: Prefers conversational interfaces for brainstorming and process documentation
Uses command-based interfaces for data queries and structured tasks
Finds conversational AI more intuitive for drafting communications

subtopics.subtopic_description: Extent to which tasks rely on specialized, tacit domain knowledge
notes: Financial reporting and compliance require deep domain expertise
Team management and coaching rely on interpersonal and organizational knowledge
AI is most effective in tasks with clear, rule-based procedures

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of trust in AI outputs for work tasks and critical decisions
notes: Trusts AI for preliminary data analysis and routine communications
Always reviews AI-generated outputs before making critical decisions
Relies on AI as a support tool, not as a final decision-maker

subtopics.subtopic_description: Ideal balance of human effort and AI automation for specific tasks
notes: Prefers AI automation for repetitive and data-heavy processes
Retains human oversight for compliance, strategy, and team management
Sees ideal workflow as AI handling routine work, freeing time for strategic input

subtopics.subtopic_description: Conditions under which high automation is acceptable or threatening
notes: High automation is acceptable when tasks are low-risk and well-defined
Prefers manual control or double-checks for tasks involving compliance or financial risk
Feels threatened by automation only if it undermines accountability or transparency

topic: AI Impact on Skills and Job Security

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived impact of AI on the importance of existing skills (enhanced vs. reduced)
notes: Believes AI enhances the importance of analytical and strategic skills
Routine data entry skills are becoming less critical due to automation
Communication and collaboration skills remain essential despite AI adoption

subtopics.subtopic_description: Emerging skills or new areas of responsibility created by AI
notes: Developing expertise in prompt engineering for effective AI use
Becoming adept at interpreting and validating AI-generated outputs
Taking responsibility for AI tool selection and workflow integration

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of concern about AI replacing specific tasks or the overall role
notes: Not concerned about AI replacing strategic or leadership responsibilities
Recognizes that some routine tasks may be fully automated in the future
Focuses on upskilling to remain relevant as automation increases

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of override mechanisms or manual checks for AI-driven processes
notes: All AI-generated reports and communications are subject to manual review
Maintains the ability to revert or correct AI-driven changes in financial systems
Trains team to always verify critical outputs from AI tools

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived change in team or company policies regarding AI adoption
notes: Company is gradually increasing AI adoption in operations
Policies emphasize human oversight and ethical AI use
Team is encouraged to pilot AI tools but maintain compliance standards

subtopics.subtopic_description: General outlook on AI's broader societal and industry impact
notes: Views AI as a positive force for efficiency and productivity
Believes AI will transform financial operations in higher education
Sees potential for AI to reduce manual workload and increase strategic focus

subtopics.subtopic_description: Personal beliefs about the ethics and risks of AI in the workplace
notes: Believes AI must be used with transparency and accountability
Concerned about potential biases in AI-generated outputs
Advocates for ethical guidelines and human review in AI deployment

subtopics.subtopic_description: Missing AI tools or features that would be most beneficial in the future
notes: Desires seamless integration between financial platforms and AI tools
Would benefit from AI that can automate complex reconciliations and compliance checks
Seeks AI features for advanced workflow automation and team management

subtopics.subtopic_description: Predicted evolution of their job in the next 5-10 years with AI integration
notes: Expects automation of most routine reporting and reconciliation tasks
Foresees a shift toward strategic financial planning and AI oversight
Anticipates increased focus on coaching, communication, and cross-functional leadership

subtopics.subtopic_description: Concrete steps they would want their organization to take regarding AI strategy
notes: Wants increased investment in AI training and upskilling for staff
Recommends implementing clear ethical standards for AI use
Advocates for pilot programs to evaluate new AI tools for finance operations
Supports regular feedback mechanisms for AI-related process improvements
